tweets.text:
If we are strictly talking about supply needs, SPR is not that important for the US. It’s more a function of supplying the rest of the world. 

The US and Canada have some of the best refineries. Excess crude from SPR can be used by refineries to export products out. 

Landlocked crude from Canada essentially makes it a form of SPR. US shale oil production has kept US crude oil production near all time highs 13.7 million b/d. 

Now we have Venezuela, which secures another 0.8-1 million b/d. 

Marginally, US gets 5-5.2 million b/d from Canada + Venezuela.

We also have access to Mexico’s heavy crude oil export (Maya), so the US is set for its refinery base in PADD 3 (Gulf). As for PADD 5 (West), that’s a different story. 

Please don’t use SPR release in the US as some mechanism for higher oil prices. The market already sees that and understands that the US doesn’t really need SPR. 

Again, SPR in the US is really to supply the rest of the world. Pricing dynamics will either shut in exports or keep the export flowing. 

On the other hand, the massive draws we are going to see in commercial oil inventories WILL push prices higher. 

Commercial > SPR
tweets.full_text:
If we are strictly talking about supply needs, SPR is not that important for the US. It’s more a function of supplying the rest of the world. 

The US and Canada have some of the best refineries. Excess crude from SPR can be used by refineries to export products out. 

Landlocked crude from Canada essentially makes it a form of SPR. US shale oil production has kept US crude oil production near all time highs 13.7 million b/d. 

Now we have Venezuela, which secures another 0.8-1 million b/d. 

Marginally, US gets 5-5.2 million b/d from Canada + Venezuela.

We also have access to Mexico’s heavy crude oil export (Maya), so the US is set for its refinery base in PADD 3 (Gulf). As for PADD 5 (West), that’s a different story. 

Please don’t use SPR release in the US as some mechanism for higher oil prices. The market already sees that and understands that the US doesn’t really need SPR. 

Again, SPR in the US is really to supply the rest of the world. Pricing dynamics will either shut in exports or keep the export flowing. 

On the other hand, the massive draws we are going to see in commercial oil inventories WILL push prices higher. 

Commercial > SPR
